---
title: "Tech Layoffs Since the Fed Began Raising Interest Rates"
subtitle: "Data Visualization: Critique and Redesign | AAI1001 – Data Engineering and Visualization"
author:
  - Liew Jia Rong (2501090)
  - Alyssa Kwek Li Yun (2403480)
  - Su Myat Nhwe (2403062)
  - Woo Wei Qin (2402934)
  - Teo Xue Ling Sharlene (2503489)
date: 2026-04-01
date-format: "dddd MMM DD, YYYY"

toc: true
toc-depth: 1
number-sections: true
number-depth: 1
engine: knitr
format:
  revealjs:
    self-contained: true
    embed-resources: true
    theme: [default]
    width: 1600
    height: 900
    margin: 0.05
    min-scale: 0.2
    max-scale: 2.0
    scrollable: false
    slide-number: true
    preview-links: false
    incremental: true
    footer: "AAI1001 – AY25/26 Tri 2 | Tech Layoffs: Critique & Redesign"
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
library(knitr)
library(tidyverse)
library(scales)

# Load data
layoffs_raw <- read_csv("layoffs_data.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

# Clean and prepare data
layoffs <- layoffs_raw |>
  # Parse dates
  mutate(Date = as.Date(Date)) |>
  # Extract year and month
  mutate(
    Year  = year(Date),
    Month = month(Date),
    YearMonth = floor_date(Date, "month")
  ) |>
  # Convert categoricals
  mutate(
    Industry = as.factor(Industry),
    Country  = as.factor(Country),
    Stage    = as.factor(Stage)
  ) |>
  # Drop rows missing both key numeric columns
  filter(!is.na(Laid_Off_Count) | !is.na(Percentage)) |>
  # Remove administrative columns not needed for visualisation
  select(-Source, -Date_Added, -List_of_Employees_Laid_Off)

# Fed rate hike reference dates
fed_hikes <- as.Date(c("2022-03-16", "2022-05-04", "2022-06-15",
                        "2022-07-27", "2022-09-21", "2022-11-02",
                        "2022-12-14", "2023-02-01", "2023-03-22",
                        "2023-05-03"))
```


# Original Visualization {.unnumbered}

## The Visualization We Analysed {.smaller}

<hr>

:::: columns
::: {.column width="58%"}
![](imgs/techlayoff.jpg){width="100%"}
:::
::: {.column width="42%"}

> **"Technology Layoffs Since the Fed Began Raising Interest Rates"**  
> Source: [proprietaryreasoning.com](https://www.proprietaryreasoning.com)  
> Coverage: January 2022 – May 2023, U.S.-based companies only (≥ 100 FTE layoffs)

**What the chart encodes:**

| Visual Channel | Variable |
|----------------|----------|
| X-axis | Date (chronological) |
| Y-axis | Industry sector |
| Bubble size | Employees laid off |
| Colour | Sector grouping |



:::
::: {.callout-note}
<span style="font-size: 1.2em;">
Selected because it directly links macroeconomic policy (Fed rate hikes) to workforce instability - a compelling, socially relevant story worth telling more clearly.
</span>
:::
::::



# Critique of the Original Design

## Strengths

<hr>

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

**1. Comprehensive Data Representation**
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #1a0909; margin-bottom: 20px;">
Displays layoffs across multiple sectors on a shared timeline, instantly showing *when* and *where* peaks occurred.
<br><br>
</div>

**2. Effective Use of Bubble Size**
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #1a0909; margin-bottom: 20px;">
Bubble size conveys layoff *magnitude*, quickly drawing attention to the major events (e.g., Meta, Amazon).
</div>

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

**3. Strategic Labeling**
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #1a0909; margin-bottom: 20px;">
Labeling only key companies provides essential real-world context without cluttering the chart.
<br><br><br>
</div>

**4. Clear Chronological Structure**
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #1a0909; margin-bottom: 20px;">
The timeline clearly connects the onset of job losses to rising interest rates, reinforcing the core narrative.
</div>

:::
::::


## Weaknesses

<hr>

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

**1. Severe Overplotting and Occlusion**
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #1a0909; margin-bottom: 20px;">
Large bubbles (e.g., Meta, Amazon) obscure smaller events. Crowded sectors like Big Tech appear as an undifferentiated mass, hiding within-sector trends.
</div>
<br>

**2. The "Area" Perception Problem**
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #1a0909; margin-bottom: 20px;">
Human perception of 2D areas is unreliable. (Cleveland & McGill, 1984). Without a size legend, viewers cannot make quantitative judgements and remains impressionistic.
</div>
<br>

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

**3. High Cognitive Load**
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #1a0909; margin-bottom: 20px;">
Viewers must simultaneously decode *position*, *size*, *colour*, and *timeline*, making it harder to extract any single insight.
</div>
<br>

**4. Poor Colour Accessibility**
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #1a0909; margin-bottom: 20px;">
Neon colors on black are inaccessible to colour-vision-deficient viewers. Adjacent overlapping bubbles cause perceptual interference, further reducing legibility.
</div>
<br>

:::
::::


# Proposed Improvements

## Our Redesign Strategy 

<hr>

Each improvement is directly mapped to a specific weakness identified to ensure our changes are purposeful and evidence-based. 

<div style="font-size: 0.85em; margin-bottom: 15px;">
| Weakness | Improvement |
|----------|-------------|
| Overplotting & Occlusion | **Small Multiples** - one panel per major industry sector |
| Area Perception Problem | **Dual encoding** - bubble size *and* colour gradient for layoff magnitude |
| High Cognitive Load | **Panel titles + event annotations** - reference lines for Fed hike dates |
| Poor Colour Accessibility | **ColorBrewer palette** on a light background; shared, labelled legend |
</div>

. . .

::: {.callout-note}
Following Tufte's principle of maximising the *data-ink ratio*, our redesign removes visual clutter without losing any information. Every layoff event becomes visible and individually legible.
:::

---

## Small Multiples Layout

<hr>

**Why Small Multiples?**

- Separating each sector into its own panel significantly reduces occlusion  
- Consistent axes allow meaningful cross-sector comparison  
- Within-sector trends become visible  

. . .

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Original approach (single crowded chart):**

- All sectors share one space  
- Larger bubbles hide smaller ones  
- Within-sector trends unreadable  

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Our approach (small multiples):**

- Each sector in its own panel  
- Every event is individually visible  
- Cross-sector comparison preserved  

:::
::::

---

## Dual Encoding for Layoff Magnitude

<hr>

**Why not rely on bubble size alone?**

- Human perception is poor at comparing 2D areas (Cleveland & McGill, 1984)  
- Overlapping bubbles distort size comparisons  

. . .

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Original approach (size only):**

- Relies entirely on area perception  
- Difficult to compare similar-sized bubbles  
- Overlap reduces interpretability  

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Our approach (dual encoding):**

- Bubble size retained for intuitive impact  
- Colour gradient adds second perceptual cue  
- Improves magnitude comparison accuracy  

:::
::::

. . .

::: {.callout-note}
Dual encoding provides redundant visual cues, improving perceptual accuracy and reducing reliance on area judgement alone.
:::

---

## Reducing Cognitive Load

<hr>

**Why is the original chart cognitively demanding?**

- Must interpret time, sector, size, and colour simultaneously  
- Requires constant cross-referencing  

. . .

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Original approach:**

- Heavy reliance on axis labels  
- Visual variables compete for attention  
- High mental effort required  

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**Our approach:**

- Panel titles replace repeated axis reading  
- Structured layout guides visual flow  
- Reduces need for cross-referencing  

:::
::::

---

## Contextual Annotations

<hr>

**Why add event annotations?**

- Layoffs are influenced by macroeconomic events  
- Without context, timing is harder to interpret  

. . .

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
**Without annotations:**

- Timeline lacks context  
- Patterns harder to interpret  
- Requires external knowledge  

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
**With annotations:**

- Vertical lines mark key events  
- Immediate economic context  
- Stronger narrative clarity  

:::
::::

. . .

::: {.callout-note}
Embedding macroeconomic context directly into the chart reduces interpretation effort and improves analytical clarity.
:::


# Data Source & Engineering


## Data Source {.smaller}

<hr>

:::: columns
::: {.column width="55%"}

::: {.fragment}
**Dataset: Kaggle — Tech Layoffs 2022**

<div style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #1a0909; margin-bottom: 12px;">
Sourced from <a href="https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/theakhilb/layoffs-data-2022" target="_blank">kaggle.com</a> — a crowdsourced compilation of verified layoff announcements drawn from public filings, news reports, and company statements.
</div>
:::

::: {.fragment}
<div style="font-size: 0.82em; color: #1a0909; margin-bottom: 4px;">

| Variable | Used in |
|----------|---------|
| `Date` | Time-series & before/after rate hike analysis |
| `Company` | Event labelling & company-level breakdown |
| `Industry` | Industry totals chart (top 12 sectors) |
| `Laid_Off_Count` | Primary magnitude across all visualisations |
| `Percentage` | Secondary magnitude; retained where count is missing |
| `Country` | Global scope; 63 countries captured |
| `Stage` | Layoffs by funding stage analysis |
| `Funds_Raised` | Correlation analysis vs. layoff count |

</div>
:::

:::
::: {.column width="45%"}

::: {.fragment}
**Why this dataset?**
:::

::: {.fragment}
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #1a0909; margin-bottom: 10px;">
**Beyond the original's scope** — the original chart was restricted to U.S. companies with ≥ 100 FTE layoffs. This dataset is global with no minimum threshold, capturing 63 countries and surfacing events the original missed entirely.
</div>
:::

::: {.fragment}
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #1a0909; margin-bottom: 10px;">
**Richer temporal window** — coverage runs from March 2020 to June 2024, giving us a full pre-hike baseline to measure against the Fed's first rate rise in March 2022.
</div>
:::

::: {.fragment}
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #1a0909; margin-bottom: 10px;">
**Analysis-ready structure** — with 3,043 cleaned records across 31 industries, the dataset is large enough for meaningful aggregation but focused enough to tell a coherent story.
</div>
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-note}
Every column in the table above maps directly to at least one chart or analysis in this presentation — no variable was loaded without purpose.
:::
:::

:::
::::


---

## Data Engineering: Pipeline & Temporal Extraction {.smaller}

<hr>

::: {.fragment}
Real-world datasets require robust, auditable transformations. We built a reproducible `dplyr` pipeline to clean the data deterministically.
:::

<br>

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {.fragment}
**1. Temporal Extraction & Parsing**
:::

::: {.fragment}
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #1a0909; margin-bottom: 12px;">
Raw dates arrived as plain character strings (e.g. `"2024-06-05"`). We cast these to proper `Date` objects to enable chronological sorting and accurate interval math.
</div>
:::

::: {.fragment}
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #1a0909; margin-bottom: 20px;">
To support our time-series aggregations, we engineered three new dimensions from each date:

- `Year` — integer year for annual grouping  
- `Month` — integer month for seasonal patterns  
- `YearMonth` — floor-rounded date for monthly bar charts  
</div>
:::

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {.fragment}
**2. Dimensionality Reduction**
:::

::: {.fragment}
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #1a0909; margin-bottom: 12px;">
The raw file contained high-cardinality administrative metadata that added noise without analytical value:
</div>
:::

::: {.fragment}
| Dropped column | Reason |
|----------------|--------|
| `Source` | External URL, not a variable |
| `Date_Added` | Log timestamp, not event date |
| `List_of_Employees_Laid_Off` | Free-text list, not parseable |
:::

::: {.fragment}
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #1a0909; margin-bottom: 20px;">
Removing these columns reduces memory overhead and ensures our pipeline operates only on data that directly encodes the story we are telling.
</div>
:::

:::
::::

---

## Data Engineering: Optimisation & Missing Values {.smaller}

<hr>

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {.fragment}
**3. Data Type Optimisation**
:::

::: {.fragment}
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #1a0909; margin-bottom: 12px;">
We cast three categorical string columns to R `factor` type:
</div>

```r
mutate(
  Industry = as.factor(Industry),
  Country  = as.factor(Country),
  Stage    = as.factor(Stage)
)
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #1a0909; margin-bottom: 20px;">
Under the hood, this maps each unique label to an integer index. The benefits are threefold: **faster `ggplot2` rendering**, **prevention of silent duplicate-level errors** caused by trailing whitespace or inconsistent capitalisation, and **correct legend ordering** in our visualisations.
</div>
:::

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {.fragment}
**4. Missing Value Handling — Listwise Deletion**
:::

::: {.fragment}
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #1a0909; margin-bottom: 12px;">
We applied a principled two-condition filter:
</div>

```r
filter(
  !is.na(Laid_Off_Count) | !is.na(Percentage)
)
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #1a0909; margin-bottom: 12px;">
A row is **retained** if it provides at least one quantitative magnitude. A row is **dropped** only if *both* metrics are absent — making it unplotable regardless.
</div>
:::

::: {.fragment}
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; color: #1a0909; margin-bottom: 20px;">
This avoids the common but wasteful practice of dropping all rows with *any* `NA`, preserving partial records that are still analytically useful.
</div>
:::

:::
::::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-note}
All four steps are chained in a single `|>` pipeline in the setup chunk — ensuring the cleaned `layoffs` object is built identically every time the document is rendered.
:::
:::

---

## Data Engineering: The Full Pipeline {.smaller}

<hr>

::: {.fragment}
The complete cleaning pipeline is fully reproducible — no manual edits, no intermediate CSVs.
:::

::: {.fragment}
```{r}
#| label: pipeline-display
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
layoffs <- layoffs_raw |>
  mutate(Date = as.Date(Date)) |>                           # Step 1a: parse dates
  mutate(
    Year      = year(Date),                                 # Step 1b: extract year
    Month     = month(Date),                                #          extract month
    YearMonth = floor_date(Date, "month")                   #          floor to month
  ) |>
  mutate(
    Industry = as.factor(Industry),                         # Step 3:  type optimisation
    Country  = as.factor(Country),
    Stage    = as.factor(Stage)
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(Laid_Off_Count) | !is.na(Percentage)) |>   # Step 4:  missing value filter
  select(-Source, -Date_Added, -List_of_Employees_Laid_Off) # Step 2:  drop noise columns
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
**Pipeline verification:**

```{r}
#| echo: false
cat("Rows remaining after deterministic cleaning:", nrow(layoffs), "\n")
cat("Temporal coverage:", 
    format(min(layoffs$Date, na.rm = TRUE)), "to", 
    format(max(layoffs$Date, na.rm = TRUE)), "\n")
cat("Industries captured:", nlevels(layoffs$Industry), "\n")
cat("Countries captured: ", nlevels(layoffs$Country))
```
:::

::: {.fragment}
::: {.callout-note}
Step ordering matters — `Year`/`Month` extraction must follow date parsing, and `select()` runs last so dropped columns cannot be referenced in earlier steps.
:::
:::



# Improved Visualization

## Small Multiples by Industry (Top 8 Sectors) {.unnumbered .unlisted}

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 16
#| fig-height: 8.2
#| fig-align: center

top_industries <- layoffs |>
  filter(!is.na(Laid_Off_Count)) |>
  group_by(Industry) |>
  summarise(Total = sum(Laid_Off_Count, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
  slice_max(Total, n = 8) |>
  pull(Industry)

sm_data <- layoffs |>
  filter(Industry %in% top_industries, !is.na(Laid_Off_Count))

ggplot(sm_data, aes(x = Date, y = Laid_Off_Count,
                    size = Laid_Off_Count, colour = Laid_Off_Count)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.65) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.numeric(fed_hikes), linetype = "dashed",
             colour = "#E15759", alpha = 0.4, linewidth = 0.4) +
  facet_wrap(~ Industry, ncol = 4, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_colour_gradient(low = "#AEC6E8", high = "#1F4E79",
                        labels = comma, name = "Laid Off") +
  scale_size_continuous(range = c(1, 8), guide = "none") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "6 months", date_labels = "%b\n%Y") +
  labs(
    title = "Layoff Events by Industry Sector - Small Multiples",
    subtitle = "Bubble size and colour intensity both encode layoff count | Red dashed lines = Fed rate hikes",
    x = NULL, y = "Employees Laid Off"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    panel.grid.minor  = element_blank(),
    strip.text        = element_text(face = "bold", size = 12),
    legend.position   = "right"
  )
```

## Observations {.smaller}

<hr>

**What the improved visualization reveals:**

- **Every event is visible** - separating sectors into panels eliminates the occlusion problem entirely. No bubble hides another.

- **Within-sector trends emerge** - e.g. Big Tech shows a sharp spike in late 2022 (Meta, Amazon), while Hardware layoffs cluster later in 2023. These patterns were completely hidden in the original.

- **Cross-sector comparison is preserved** - consistent x-axes across all panels allow viewers to compare timing and scale between industries at a glance.

- **Magnitude is doubly encoded** - both bubble size *and* colour intensity represent layoff count, giving two independent perceptual cues for the same variable and reducing reliance on area judgement alone.

- **Macroeconomic context is embedded** - red dashed vertical lines mark each Federal Reserve interest rate hike date, directly connecting the data to the economic narrative without requiring viewers to recall external context.

- **Accessible design** - light background with a perceptually uniform blue gradient replaces the original neon-on-black palette, improving readability for all viewers including those with colour vision deficiency.


# Data Analysis

## Time-Series: Before vs. After Rate Hikes {.smaller}

<hr>

```{r}
#| echo: false

hike_start <- as.Date("2022-03-16")

before_after <- layoffs |>
  filter(!is.na(Laid_Off_Count)) |>
  mutate(Period = if_else(Date < hike_start, "Before Hikes", "After Hikes")) |>
  group_by(Period) |>
  summarise(
    Total_Laid_Off  = sum(Laid_Off_Count, na.rm = TRUE),
    Avg_Monthly     = round(sum(Laid_Off_Count, na.rm = TRUE) /
                              n_distinct(floor_date(Date, "month")), 0),
    Events          = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

before_after |>
  mutate(across(c(Total_Laid_Off, Avg_Monthly), comma)) |>
  knitr::kable(
    col.names = c("Period", "Total Laid Off", "Avg / Month", "Events"),
    align = "lrrr"
  )
```

. . .

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 3.2

monthly_period <- layoffs |>
  filter(!is.na(Laid_Off_Count)) |>
  mutate(Period = if_else(Date < hike_start, "Before Hikes", "After Hikes")) |>
  group_by(YearMonth, Period) |>
  summarise(Total = sum(Laid_Off_Count, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

ggplot(monthly_period, aes(x = YearMonth, y = Total, fill = Period)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Before Hikes" = "#AEC6E8", "After Hikes" = "#1F4E79")) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma) +
  scale_x_date(date_breaks = "3 months", date_labels = "%b %Y") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = as.numeric(hike_start), colour = "#E15759",
             linewidth = 1, linetype = "solid") +
  labs(x = NULL, y = "Employees Laid Off", fill = "Period") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

> Monthly layoff volumes after March 2022 are substantially higher than the pre-hike baseline, providing quantitative support for the original chart's core narrative.


## Layoffs by Industry - Totals

<hr>

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 13
#| fig-height: 5.2

industry_summary <- layoffs |>
  filter(!is.na(Laid_Off_Count)) |>
  group_by(Industry) |>
  summarise(Total = sum(Laid_Off_Count, na.rm = TRUE),
            Events = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  slice_max(Total, n = 12) |>
  mutate(Industry = fct_reorder(Industry, Total))

ggplot(industry_summary, aes(x = Total, y = Industry, fill = Total)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = comma(Total)), hjust = -0.1, size = 3.5) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "#AEC6E8", high = "#1F4E79") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma,
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  labs(
    title = "Total Employees Laid Off by Industry (Top 12)",
    x = "Total Employees Laid Off", y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())
```

> Consumer tech and retail sectors bear the heaviest absolute burden, while sectors like Legal and Infrastructure show comparatively limited exposure.


## Layoffs by Company Stage

<hr>

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 13
#| fig-height: 4.8

stage_data <- layoffs |>
  filter(!is.na(Laid_Off_Count), !is.na(Stage),
         Stage != "Unknown", Stage != "") |>
  group_by(Stage) |>
  summarise(
    Total       = sum(Laid_Off_Count, na.rm = TRUE),
    Mean_Layoff = mean(Laid_Off_Count, na.rm = TRUE),
    Events      = n(),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(Stage = fct_reorder(Stage, Total))

ggplot(stage_data, aes(x = Total, y = Stage)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#76B7B2", width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(comma(Total), " (", Events, " events)")),
            hjust = -0.05, size = 3.5) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = comma,
                     expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.25))) +
  labs(
    title = "Total Layoffs by Company Funding Stage",
    subtitle = "Post-IPO firms account for the largest share of total layoffs",
    x = "Total Employees Laid Off", y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
  theme(panel.grid.major.y = element_blank())
```

> Post-IPO companies - facing public market pressure - drove the most layoffs, suggesting publicly listed firms responded most aggressively to tightening financial conditions.


## Funds Raised vs. Layoff Count

<hr>

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| fig-width: 12
#| fig-height: 4.8

corr_data <- layoffs |>
  filter(!is.na(Laid_Off_Count), !is.na(Funds_Raised),
         Funds_Raised > 0, Laid_Off_Count > 0)

cor_val <- cor(corr_data$Funds_Raised, corr_data$Laid_Off_Count, use = "complete.obs")

ggplot(corr_data, aes(x = Funds_Raised, y = Laid_Off_Count)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.35, colour = "#4E79A7", size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", colour = "#E15759", se = TRUE, linewidth = 1) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = comma) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = comma) +
  labs(
    title = "Funds Raised vs. Layoff Count (log–log scale)",
    subtitle = paste0("Pearson r = ", round(cor_val, 3),
                      " - companies with more capital tend to have larger absolute layoffs"),
    x = "Funds Raised (USD millions, log scale)",
    y = "Employees Laid Off (log scale)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```

> A modest positive correlation exists - larger, better-funded companies tend to report larger absolute layoffs, consistent with the scale of their workforces.


# Insights & Conclusion

## Key Insights

<hr>

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Temporal Escalation**
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; margin-bottom: 20px;">
Layoffs surged significantly *after* the first rate hike in March 2022, empirically supporting the original chart's core narrative.
</div>
<br>

**Sector Vulnerability**
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; margin-bottom: 20px;">
Consumer tech, retail, and finance sectors show the highest absolute layoff counts. Our small multiples reveal that the *timing* of layoff peaks also differs by sector - a nuance entirely hidden in the original.
</div>
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

**Stage Matters**
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; margin-bottom: 20px;">
Public, post-IPO companies drove the majority of layoffs, appear to respond aggressively to tightening conditions and quarterly earnings pressures.
</div>
<br>

**Capital & Scale**
<div style="font-size: 0.85em; margin-bottom: 20px;">
Layoffs positively correlate with funds raised. This reflects scale rather than recklessness: larger firms simply had more jobs to cut.
</div>

:::
::::


## Conclusion {.smaller}

<hr>

> Our single redesigned visualization - a **faceted small multiples scatter plot** - replaces the original cluttered bubble chart, making every layoff event visible, legible, and comparable across sectors.

. . .

**What we improved:**

- Overplotting eliminated via panel-per-sector layout
- Magnitude judgement aided by dual encoding (size + colour gradient)
- Cognitive load reduced through panel titles and Fed hike annotations
- Accessibility improved with ColorBrewer palette on a light background

. . .

**What the data tells us:**

The Federal Reserve's 2022 rate hike cycle coincided with a dramatic and sustained escalation in tech-sector layoffs globally - a pattern that is only clearly visible once the visualization is redesigned to let the data breathe.

. . .

::: {.callout-note}
All source code, cleaned data, and rendered HTML outputs are included in the project ZIP package for full reproducibility.
:::


# Appendix {.unnumbered}

## Team Contributions {.smaller}

<hr>

| Team Member | Contributions |
|-------------|--------------|
| **Liew Jia Rong** | Lead on data engineering, responsible for dataset cleaning, transformation and source documentation. |
| **Alyssa Kwek Li Yun** | Developed the project narrative, synthesized final insights and conclusions. |
| **Su Myat Nhwe** | Executed the improved visualization and in-depth data analysis, focusing on trend validation. |
| **Woo Wei Qin** | Outlined the proposed design improvements and supported the early-stage data exploration. |
| **Teo Xue Ling Sharlene** | Conducted the design critique, evaluating the effectiveness and limitations of the original visualization. |
| **All Members** | Collaborative preparation of the RevealJS presentation and final MP4 recording. |


## References {.unnumbered}

<hr>

- Cleveland, W. S., & McGill, R. (1984). Graphical perception: Theory, experimentation, and application to the development of graphical methods. *Journal of the American Statistical Association, 79*(387), 531–554.

- Tufte, E. R. (2001). *The Visual Display of Quantitative Information* (2nd ed.). Graphics Press.

- Layoffs dataset: [Kaggle - Layoffs Data 2022](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/theakhilb/layoffs-data-2022)

- ColorBrewer: [colorbrewer2.org](https://colorbrewer2.org)
